food_name: Teochew Steamed Fish (Pomfret or Snapper with Preserved Vegetables and Salted Plum)
aliases: steamed fish with salted plum and chai poh

english: steamed fish with salted plum and chai poh

region_of_origin: Chaoshan (Teochew), Guangdong, China; widely served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Steamed whole fish with preserved mustard greens, salted plum, tomato, ginger, and glass noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Teochew Steamed Fish. Closest parent is steamed fish with preserved vegetable accompaniment. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed whole fish (pomfret or snapper) with Chinese preserved mustard greens, salted plum, tomato, and ginger — Teochew style
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A022H is the closest FoodEx2 parent for steamed fish preparations; no exact Teochew-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Teochew Steamed Fish as a specific food entity.

main_ingredients: white pomfret or red snapper (whole fish)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200g, representing one quarter of a whole steamed pomfret or snapper together with its preserved vegetable, salted plum, tomato, ginger, and glass noodle accompaniments.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Teochew Steamed Fish; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Pomfret / Snapper (raw, steamed); component values for chai poh and salted plum from HPB Singapore nutrient database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core fish macros (protein, fat, energy) anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for steamed pomfret/snapper. Sodium elevated by chai poh (~1200 mg/100g) and salted plum (~800 mg/100g) components per HPB Singapore nutrient database. Composite dish values cross-referenced with HPB Foodo entry for Teochew Steamed Fish. Selenium value (42 mcg/100g) is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for pomfret. All values represent the edible composite dish per 100g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli) GI ~26; fish and preserved vegetables contribute negligible carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: The dominant carbohydrate source in Teochew Steamed Fish is the glass noodle base (mung bean vermicelli), which has a published GI of approximately 26 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Fish, preserved vegetables, tomato, and ginger contribute negligible digestible carbohydrate. The composite dish GI is estimated at 26 (low). GL per 200g serving = (26 × 9.6g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.5, confirming very low glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (585 mg/100g; 1170 mg per 200g serving) from chai poh (preserved salted mustard greens) and salted plum
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request chai poh and salted plum on the side or in reduced quantity, as a standard 200g serving delivers approximately 1170 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — and this dish has significantly higher sodium than Cantonese steamed fish due to its preserved accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load per serving (GL 2.5); protein-dominant dish with low digestible carbohydrate; glass noodles are low GI (~26)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Teochew Steamed Fish is an excellent choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, with a glycemic load of only 2.5 per 200g serving, high lean protein (27g per serving), and negligible carbohydrate contribution from fish and preserved vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable lipid profile (low saturated fat 0.5g/100g, omega-3 from pomfret/snapper, high selenium 42 mcg/100g) offset by high sodium from preserved accompaniments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lean fish base provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, very low saturated fat, and high selenium, making it a heart-healthy protein choice, but the high sodium content from chai poh and salted plum warrants caution for patients with cardiovascular disease who require sodium restriction; requesting reduced preserved vegetables is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Excellent anti-inflammatory profile: very high selenium (42 mcg/100g), vitamin D (4.8 mcg/100g), omega-3 from fish, lycopene from tomato, very low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Teochew Steamed Fish provides one of the highest selenium values among Singapore dishes and meaningful vitamin D from pomfret, both of which support anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teochew Steamed Fish (潮州蒸魚) is one of the most elegant and distinctively flavoured dishes of Teochew cuisine, widely served at Teochew Chinese restaurants, family banquets, and zi char stalls in Singapore. A whole white pomfret (chang yue) or red snapper is steamed on a plate arranged with sliced chai poh (preserved salted mustard greens), whole salted plum (sng buay), sliced fresh tomato, thin slices of fresh ginger, and a nest of soaked glass noodles at the base to absorb the steaming juices. After steaming, the dish is finished with sesame oil and light soy sauce. The result is a delicate, clean-tasting fish with a sour, savoury, umami complexity from the preserved vegetables and salted plum that distinguishes it sharply from the simpler Cantonese version. Nutritionally, it is one of the healthiest Chinese fish preparations: very lean (2.5g fat/100g), high in lean protein (13.5g/100g), rich in selenium (42 mcg/100g — among the highest in the Singapore food database), and a meaningful source of vitamin D (4.8 mcg/100g) and vitamin B12 (1.5 mcg/100g). The glycemic load per 200g serving is only 2.5, making it excellent for blood glucose management. The primary clinical caution is its high sodium content (585 mg/100g; 1170 mg per serving) from the preserved mustard greens and salted plum, which is significantly higher than Cantonese steamed fish and requires attention for patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: Teochew vs Cantonese steamed fish nutrition
steamed fish with salted plum calories

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients are anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for steamed pomfret/snapper. Sodium values are consistent with known composition of chai poh and salted plum from HPB nutrient database. Selenium value is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for pomfret. GI is estimated from the glass noodle component using the University of Sydney GI Database. All values are internally consistent and cross-referenced across two approved primary sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Teochew steamed fish dish is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026; omega-3 presence is qualitatively confirmed from pomfret/snapper but quantitative split is unavailable. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for a steamed fish preparation with no hydrogenated fats. GI of 26 is an analogous estimate based on the glass noodle (mung bean vermicelli) component as the dominant carbohydrate source, per University of Sydney GI Database; fish, preserved vegetables, tomato, and ginger contribute negligible digestible carbohydrate. Sodium is notably high (585 mg/100g) due to chai poh and salted plum; this is a defining characteristic of Teochew-style versus Cantonese-style steamed fish. Selenium value of 42 mcg/100g is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for pomfret and represents one of the highest selenium values among Singapore dishes in this database.
